Read three headline numbers together. ranges 0 1 rises daily pattern repeats exactly day day; IV runs 0 (smooth sine) 2 (noisy split ultradian bouts); RA ranges 0 1 grows contrast active day restful night. high RA (0.98) sits low (0.23): strong day-night contrast carried irregular timing. header also notes six eight calendar days qualified, circadian.rhythm() sets aside days little valid recording computing metrics.
code: rhythm <- circadian.rhythm(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) rhythm #>  #> Circadian Rhythm Analysis #>  #> Data Summary #>   Days analyzed:            8 #>   Valid circadian days:     6 #>   Epoch length:             60 seconds #>  #> Non-Parametric Metrics (IS/IV: Witting et al. 1990; RA/L5/M10: van Someren et al. 1999) #>   L5 (least active 5h):     6.10 counts/min, onset 01:28 #>   M10 (most active 10h):    602.93 counts/min, onset 12:02 #>   L1 (least active 1h):     3.10 counts/min, onset 02:35 #>   M1 (most active 1h):      1175.51 counts/min, onset 17:43 #>   Relative Amplitude (RA):  0.9800 (range 0-1, higher=stronger rhythm) #>   Interdaily Stability (IS): 0.2279 (range 0-1, higher=more consistent) #>   Intradaily Variability (IV): 1.0008 (near 0 = sine, near 2 = noise) #>   Phi (autocorrelation):    0.4151 (higher=more predictable) #>  #> Sleep-Based & Variability Metrics #>   Sleep Regularity Index:   Not calculated (requires sleep_state input) #>   Onset timing variability: 2.23 hours #>   L5 timing variability:    0.77 hours (circular SD) #>   M10 timing variability:   3.70 hours (circular SD) #>  #> References: Witting (1990), van Someren (1999)

Overlay fit see catching. Hourly activity fitted 24-hour cosine overlaid; peak curve acrophase height MESOR line amplitude. Systematic departures curve single cosine smooth real rest-activity data. code: cos <- cosinor.analysis(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, period = 24) c(mesor = unname(cos$mesor), amplitude = unname(cos$amplitude),   acrophase = unname(cos$acrophase)) #>     mesor amplitude acrophase  #>    327.25    295.63     16.92 rhythmicity.test(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, cosinor_result = cos) #> Cosinor Rhythmicity Test (Halberg zero-amplitude F-test) #>  #> H0: amplitude = 0 (no rhythm) #>   Period:         24 h #>   F(2, 21):        8.15 #>   P-value:        0.0024 #>   Percent rhythm: 43.7% (R-squared = 0.4371) #>   Rhythmic:       YES (alpha = 0.05) plot_extended_cosinor(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)

Passing seed = 1 makes bootstrap reproducible; 50 replicates keep vignette fast, real interval use . Lomb-Scargle spectral power across candidate periods; tallest peak dominant rhythm, peak rising significance line rejects -rhythm null. chi-square periodogram Sokolove Bushell (1978) asks question different way, scoring trial period much variance folds onto . second estimator clears threshold period reassurance peak real rather artifact one method. Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) periodogram: Qp statistic trial period, significance threshold. peak fall period Lomb-Scargle periodogram found.
code: per <- circadian.period(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) c(tau = per$tau, p_value = per$p_value) #>          tau      p_value  #> 2.543077e+01 9.388299e-97 period.ci(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, n_boot = 50, seed = 1) #> Circadian Period with Bootstrap Confidence Interval #>  #>   tau:      24.980 h #>   95% CI:   [23.484, 26.342] h #>   SE:       1.220 h #>   Method:   circular block residual bootstrap (50/50 valid reps) plot_periodogram(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) plot_chisq(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: cosinor fits one cosine day. circadian.flm() fits functional model 24-hour profile several Fourier harmonics, follows real, asymmetric shape day instead forcing symmetric curve (Wang et al. 2011); single cosine one-harmonic special case. functional fit (line) averaged hourly profile (points). extra Fourier harmonics let curve follow asymmetric morning rise evening decline single cosine smooths . extra harmonics buy back variance single cosine left table, curve now bends morning rise one cosine ran straight past. circadian.ssa() takes opposite view. Instead average day decomposes whole recording singular spectrum analysis additive components: slow trend, circadian pair, noise (Golyandina Zhigljavsky 2013). trajectory matrix grows series, bin long minute recording coarser epoch first. circadian component singular spectrum analysis pulls whole recording: daily rhythm separated slow trend noise. recording circadian pair carries 14 percent variance fundamental period near 24.5 hours, slightly-long period periodogram found earlier, now isolated clean component raw series.
code: flm <- circadian.flm(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) flm #> Functional Linear Model (24-hour activity profile) #>  #>   Basis:        fourier (order 4, 9 terms) #>   Period:       24 hours #>   Days / profile points: 8 / 24 #>  #>   Model fit: #>     R-squared:   0.7352 #>     AIC:         406.84 #>     F-statistic: 5.21 (p = 0.003) #>  #>   Dominant harmonic: H1, amplitude 303.40, acrophase 16.99 h #>  #>   Peak 956.3 at 19.2 h; trough -117.7 at 3.8 h #>  #>   Reference: Wang et al. (2011) ggplot(flm$smooth_curve, aes(t, activity)) +   geom_point(data = flm$fitted_profile, aes(t, observed), colour = "grey55") +   geom_line(colour = "#236192", linewidth = 1) +   labs(x = "Hour of day", y = "Activity") +   theme_actiRhythm() bin      <- as.integer(as.numeric(agd$timestamp) %/% 600)   # 10-minute bins counts10 <- tapply(agd$axis1, bin, sum) t10      <- as.POSIXct(as.numeric(names(counts10)) * 600, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC") ssa <- circadian.ssa(as.numeric(counts10), t10) ssa #> Singular Spectrum Analysis (Basic SSA) #>  #>   Window length L:    144 epochs (24.0 h) #>   Series length n:    993 (K = 850, span 6.9 days) #>   Components kept:     10 of rank 144 #>  #>   Variance explained (leading components): #>     ET1   lambda = 0.2929  (cumulative 0.2929) #>     ET2   lambda = 0.0814  (cumulative 0.3743) #>     ET3   lambda = 0.0578  (cumulative 0.4321) #>     ET4   lambda = 0.0295  (cumulative 0.4616) #>     ET5   lambda = 0.0293  (cumulative 0.4909) #>  #>   Grouping: #>     Trend:      components 1 #>     Circadian:  components 2, 3 (13.9% of variance) #>     Fundamental period: 24.49 h #>  #>   Reference: Golyandina and Zhigljavsky (2013) ggplot(data.frame(time = t10, circadian = ssa$circadian), aes(time, circadian)) +   geom_line(colour = "#236192") +   labs(x = "Time", y = "Circadian component") +   theme_actiRhythm()

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: Everything far runs activity counts. Raw accelerometer files also record gravity component, body posture, counts carry. actiRhythm reads raw ActiGraph .gt3x, Axivity .cwa, GENEActiv .bin files, auto-calibrates , derives three raw metrics used across field: ENMO, MAD, z-angle. raw.metrics() takes file path. Raw acceleration far large bundle, reproducible illustration example_raw() synthesises recording; real file read way, raw.metrics("recording.cwa"). ENMO activity signal, every method takes directly: pass circadian.rhythm(), let circadian.raw() run whole analysis file. Calibration applied first inside raw.metrics(). auto.calibrate() finds per-axis gain offset return still periods 1 g sphere (van Hees 2014); data known distortion returns original gain offset within fraction percent. z-angle supports sleep detector needs diary works posture, counts . rest.spt() finds nightly sleep-period-time window distribution angle change (HDCZA, van Hees 2018), sib.vanhees() scores sustained-inactivity bouts (van Hees 2015), sleep..spt() combines onset, wake efficiency. detect.nonwear.raw() flags stationary, taken-device (low standard deviation range hour, van Hees 2011), passing mask wear keeps device-stretch read one long night. Computed synthetic recording 5-second epoch, detected windows shaded. z-angle synthetic recording 5-second epochs, two nightly sleep-period-time windows detected angle alone (HDCZA), gated raw non-wear, shaded. Two estimators measure fragmentation, run counts already loaded: activity.balance.index() maps detrended-fluctuation exponent 0 1 score peaks healthy 1/f balance (Danilevicz 2024), transition.probability() gives closed-form rest--active active--rest transition probabilities.
code: raw <- example_raw(days = 2)        # synthetic 2-day recording (or a real file path) m <- raw.metrics(raw, epoch = 60)   # per-epoch ENMO (mg), MAD, and the z-angle head(m) #>                  time     ENMO      MAD    anglez #> 1 2024-01-01 12:00:00 43.95883 24.78791  8.280445 #> 2 2024-01-01 12:01:00 43.13860 24.89003 14.666095 #> 3 2024-01-01 12:02:00 44.56626 25.55921  8.287697 #> 4 2024-01-01 12:03:00 44.25995 24.90778 14.705513 #> 5 2024-01-01 12:04:00 44.79032 25.36485  8.292428 #> 6 2024-01-01 12:05:00 44.17071 24.72920 14.755796 cr <- circadian.rhythm(m$ENMO, m$time) c(IS = cr$IS, IV = cr$IV, RA = cr$RA) #>     IS     IV     RA  #> 1.0000 0.3823 0.9873 set.seed(1) u <- matrix(rnorm(40 * 3), 40, 3); u <- u / sqrt(rowSums(u^2)) cal <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(40), function(i)   matrix(rep(u[i, ] / c(1.03, 0.97, 1.01) + c(0.04, -0.03, 0.02), each = 300),          300, 3) + rnorm(900, 0, 0.004))) auto.calibrate(data.frame(x = cal[, 1], y = cal[, 2], z = cal[, 3]),                fs = 30)[c("scale", "offset")] #> $scale #> [1] 1.0301368 0.9701036 1.0099054 #>  #> $offset #> [1]  0.04000814 -0.02992012  0.01990532 m5    <- raw.metrics(raw, epoch = 5, metrics = "anglez") wear  <- detect.nonwear.raw(raw, epoch = 5) spt   <- rest.spt(m5$anglez, m5$time, epoch_length = 5, wear = wear) sib   <- sib.vanhees(m5$anglez, epoch_length = 5) sleep <- sleep.from.spt(spt, sib, m5$time, epoch_length = 5) thin <- seq(1, nrow(m5), by = 12) ggplot() +   geom_rect(data = spt, aes(xmin = onset, xmax = offset, ymin = -90, ymax = 90),             fill = "#236192", alpha = 0.18) +   geom_line(data = m5[thin, ], aes(time, anglez), linewidth = 0.2, colour = "grey30") +   labs(x = NULL, y = "z-angle (deg)") +   theme_actiRhythm() sleep[, c("date", "onset", "offset", "tst", "efficiency")] #>         date               onset              offset      tst efficiency #> 1 2024-01-01 2024-01-01 23:00:00 2024-01-02 07:00:00 8.001389  0.9968853 #> 2 2024-01-02 2024-01-02 23:00:00 2024-01-03 07:00:00 8.001389  0.9968853 activity.balance.index(fractal.dfa(agd$axis1)) #> $ABI_overall #> [1] 0.9900827 #>  #> $ABI_short #> [1] 0.9943423 #>  #> $ABI_long #> [1] 0.9820051 transition.probability(agd$axis1)[c("tp_ra_mle", "tp_ar_mle")] #> $tp_ra_mle #> [1] 0.04853705 #>  #> $tp_ar_mle #> [1] 0.1366603

code: library(GGIR) P <- load_params(); P$params_general$windowsizes <- c(5, 900, 3600) I <- g.inspectfile(file, params_rawdata = P$params_rawdata, params_general = P$params_general) ggir <- g.getmeta(file, params_rawdata = P$params_rawdata, params_general = P$params_general,                   params_cleaning = P$params_cleaning, inspectfileobject = I)$metashort acti <- raw.metrics(file, epoch = 5)  # Align on wall-clock time: the .gt3x stores local time and the two tools anchor it # slightly differently, so match the clock label rather than absolute seconds. gkey <- gsub("T", " ", substr(as.character(ggir$timestamp), 1, 19)) akey <- format(acti$time, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") m <- merge(data.frame(k = gkey, ge = ggir$ENMO * 1000, ga = ggir$anglez),            data.frame(k = akey, ae = acti$ENMO,        aa = acti$anglez), by = "k") c(ENMO = cor(m$ge, m$ae), anglez = cor(m$ga, m$aa))

title: Nonstationary and complex rhythms
text: nonstationary file bundle, synthesise one: fourteen days one-minute epochs, active block day quiet nights, whose peak advances two half hours earlier across day 7, whose nights fragment recording goes . methods locate per-day landmarks read minute series directly; heavier decompositions read ten-minute binned copy, split getting-started vignette uses. double-plotted actogram tells story glance. active band sits clock time first week, steps left around day 7 peak moves earlier, later rows scatter nights fragment. Double-plotted actogram synthetic recording. activity band advances two half hours earlier around day 7, later days fragment. single cosine fit fourteen days represent either change.
code: set.seed(42) days <- 14 t    <- as.POSIXct("2024-03-04", tz = "UTC") + (seq_len(days * 1440) - 1) * 60 hour <- as.numeric(format(t, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(t, "%M")) / 60 day  <- (seq_along(t) - 1) %/% 1440 + 1  acro  <- 15 - 2.5 * pmin(pmax(day - 7, 0), 1)            # peak advances over day 7 block <- plogis(4 * cos(2 * pi * (hour - acro) / 24))    # active by day, quiet at night frag  <- (day - 1) / (days - 1)                          # 0 to 1 across the recording counts <- rpois(length(t), 5 + 300 * block) night_break <- runif(length(t)) < 0.10 * frag & block < 0.2 counts[night_break] <- rpois(sum(night_break), 150)      # nights fragment late on  agg      <- tapply(counts, as.integer(as.numeric(t) %/% 600), sum)   # 10-minute bins counts10 <- as.numeric(agg) t10      <- as.POSIXct(as.numeric(names(agg)) * 600, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC") plot_actogram(counts, t, scale = "sqrt")

code: ssa <- circadian.ssa(counts10, t10) parts <- rbind(   data.frame(t = t10, value = ssa$circadian, part = "circadian"),   data.frame(t = t10, value = ssa$trend,     part = "trend"),   data.frame(t = t10, value = ssa$residual,  part = "residual")) parts$part <- factor(parts$part, c("circadian", "trend", "residual")) ggplot(parts, aes(t, value)) +   geom_line(colour = "#236192", linewidth = 0.3) +   facet_wrap(~part, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +   labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme_actiRhythm() emd <- circadian.emd(counts, t) hht <- hilbert.huang(emd) ggplot(data.frame(t = emd$times, period = hht$period), aes(t, period)) +   geom_line(colour = "#236192") +   geom_hline(yintercept = 24, linetype = 2, colour = "grey50") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(18, 30)) +   labs(x = NULL, y = "Instantaneous period (h)") +   theme_actiRhythm() #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_line()`).

title: Nonstationary and complex rhythms
text: Fragmentation change structure, just level, shows multiscale measures. Splitting recording clean first half broken second half, late half carries sample entropy narrower multifractal spectrum, signs dynamics simplified toward noise: less predictable epoch epoch, less richly self-similar across scales. Sample entropy coarse-graining scale two halves. fragmented second half carries entropy, less predictable broken rhythm.
code: half  <- length(counts10) %/% 2 early <- counts10[1:half]; late <- counts10[(half + 1):length(counts10)] mse_e <- multiscale.entropy(early, scales = 1:10) mse_l <- multiscale.entropy(late,  scales = 1:10) c(early_complexity = mse_e$area, late_complexity = mse_l$area) #> early_complexity  late_complexity  #>         2.937170         3.327456 c(early_mf_width = mfdfa(early)$width, late_mf_width = mfdfa(late)$width) #> early_mf_width  late_mf_width  #>       1.654179       1.084617 mse <- rbind(data.frame(scale = mse_e$scales, sampen = mse_e$mse, half = "first half"),              data.frame(scale = mse_l$scales, sampen = mse_l$mse, half = "second half")) ggplot(mse, aes(scale, sampen, colour = half)) +   geom_line() + geom_point(size = 1.5) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("first half" = "#236192", "second half" = "#E69F00")) +   labs(x = "Scale factor", y = "Sample entropy", colour = NULL) +   theme_actiRhythm()

Double-plotted actogram: row one day shown twice across 48 hours, stable rhythm forms vertical band activity clock time day, scattered fill marks fragmented shifting rhythm. recording high relative amplitude near 0.98 sits low interdaily stability near 0.23: days strongly active, pattern land clock time one day next. code: library(actiRhythm)  # Read the bundled .agd and pull the count series agd <- agd.counts(read.agd(example_agd(1), verbose = FALSE))  # Describe the rest-activity rhythm without assuming a waveform rhythm <- circadian.rhythm(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) rhythm #>  #> Circadian Rhythm Analysis #>  #> Data Summary #>   Days analyzed:            8 #>   Valid circadian days:     6 #>   Epoch length:             60 seconds #>  #> Non-Parametric Metrics (IS/IV: Witting et al. 1990; RA/L5/M10: van Someren et al. 1999) #>   L5 (least active 5h):     6.10 counts/min, onset 01:28 #>   M10 (most active 10h):    602.93 counts/min, onset 12:02 #>   L1 (least active 1h):     3.10 counts/min, onset 02:35 #>   M1 (most active 1h):      1175.51 counts/min, onset 17:43 #>   Relative Amplitude (RA):  0.9800 (range 0-1, higher=stronger rhythm) #>   Interdaily Stability (IS): 0.2279 (range 0-1, higher=more consistent) #>   Intradaily Variability (IV): 1.0008 (~0=sine, ~2=noise) #>   Phi (autocorrelation):    0.4151 (higher=more predictable) #>  #> Sleep-Based & Variability Metrics #>   Sleep Regularity Index:   Not calculated (requires sleep_state input) #>   Onset timing variability: 2.23 hours #>   L5 timing variability:    0.77 hours (circular SD) #>   M10 timing variability:   3.70 hours (circular SD) #>  #> References: Witting (1990), van Someren (1999) plot_actogram(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)

title: Circadian Rest-Activity Rhythm Analysis
text: recording runs rest package. cosinor.analysis() fits 24-hour cosine (Cornelissen 2014) rhythmicity.test() checks whether rhythm statistically real. circadian.period() estimates free-running period Lomb-Scargle periodogram (Lomb 1976), period.ci() puts bootstrap confidence interval , circadian.spectrogram() shows period drifts across recording. fractal.dfa() measures long-range correlation series (Peng 1994), multiscale.entropy() mfdfa() describe finer nonlinear structure. circadian.flm() fits functional (multi-harmonic) model daily profile circadian.ssa() decomposes recording trend, circadian, noise components. sleep.regularity.index() (Phillips 2017), social.jet.lag(), state.transitions() summarise sleep timing rest-activity fragmentation, sleep.changepoints() locates night’s sleep wake onset directly counts (Chen Sun 2024). sleep.cole.kripke() sleep.sadeh() score epoch sleep wake (Cole et al. 1992; Sadeh et al. 1994), rest.periods() (Roenneberg et al. 2015) rest.crespo() (Crespo et al. 2012) consolidate every rest bout across recording, naps included, two independent algorithms. set methods covers harder cases. circadian.wavelet() gives time-frequency power surface, ultradian.bandpower() dyadic ultradian bands, circadian.emd() hilbert.huang() data-adaptive decomposition instantaneous phase. cosinor.multicomponent() fits multi-harmonic shape, activity.onset.offset() marks daily onset offset, phase.concentration() tests day--day phase clustering. rest.hmm() state-space rest-activity model, curve.registration() aligns daily profiles scale-invariant chronotype phase, residual.spectrum() spectra cosinor leaves behind. circadian.daily(), intradaily.variability.multiscale(), activity.extrema(), dichotomy.index() complete nonparametric set. Counts can come .agd files: read.actigraph.csv() reads ActiLife epoch CSVs counts..data.frame() takes count series data frame. read.raw() turns raw ActiGraph .gt3x, Axivity .cwa, GENEActiv .bin files counts agcounts band-pass filter, gt3x.counts(), axivity.counts(), geneactiv.counts() per brand. Cross-brand counts approximation rather native ActiGraph output. detect.nonwear.choi() detect.nonwear.troiano() flag non-wear time directly counts use wear filter (Choi et al. 2011; Troiano et al. 2008). Raw acceleration also gives gravity-based metrics counts carry. raw.metrics() returns per-epoch ENMO, MAD z-angle van Hees (2014) auto-calibration, circadian.raw() runs every method ENMO single call. z-angle also supports sleep pipeline needs diary, counts . rest.spt() finds nightly sleep-period window (HDCZA, van Hees 2018) sib.vanhees() scores sustained-inactivity bouts (van Hees 2015); sleep..spt() turns onset, wake, WASO efficiency. detect.nonwear.raw() gates device taken left still, read sleep (van Hees 2011). activity.balance.index() transition.probability() add two fragmentation estimators. study many files, circadian.batch() runs whole pipeline folder returns one row per recording, circadian.workbook() writes full analysis multi-sheet Excel file. Every plotting function returns ggplot object can theme save save.circadian.plot().

text: named list period (hours, \(Q_P\) peak), Qp_peak, p_value (family-wise Sidak p-value peak across scanned periods), significant (logical, p_value < alpha), scanned (trial periods hours), Qp (periodogram aligned scanned), critical (per-period chi-square threshold), epoch_length alpha. insufficient/degenerate data shape returned period/Qp_peak NA empty vectors; function never throws.

title: Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) Periodogram — chi.sq.periodogram
text: trial period \(P\) (integer number epochs) series folded \(P\) phase bins \(K = \lfloor N/P \rfloor\) complete cycles statistic $$Q_P = \frac{N \sum_{h=1}^{P}(\bar{}_h - \bar{})^2}{\sum_{=1}^{N}(A_i - \bar{})^2}$$ computed, \(\bar{}_h\) mean phase \(h\) \(\bar{}\) grand mean \(N = KP\) retained points. null hypothesis rhythm \(P\), \(Q_P\) follows chi-square distribution \(P-1\) degrees freedom, giving significance line \(\chi^2_{P-1,\,1-\alpha}\). estimated period \(P\) maximising \(Q_P\) within search window.

title: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
text: Processing steps: Timestamps converted hours since first sample (t_hours = (.numeric(timestamps) - min) / 3600). Pairs missing count missing/non-finite time dropped. title: Circadian Rhythm Analysis — circadian.rhythm
text: Non-Parametric Metrics (van Someren et al., 1999): L5/M10 use minute-level sliding window, hourly aggregation, timing resolved minute rather hour. L5 = Average activity least active 5 consecutive hours. Uses sliding window across minute-level data precise timing. M10 = Average activity active 10 consecutive hours. RA = Relative amplitude = (M10 - L5) / (M10 + L5). Ranges 0 1; higher values mean stronger day-night amplitude. = Interdaily stability. Measures coupling rhythm stable zeitgebers. Range 0 (Gaussian noise) 1 (perfect stability). IV = Intradaily variability. Measures rhythm fragmentation. 0 perfect sine wave, 2 Gaussian noise, can exceed 2 ultradian rhythms. Sleep Regularity Index (Phillips et al., 2017): SRI = probability sleep/wake state two time points 24 hours apart. Range -100 +100, 100 indicating perfect regularity.

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: Fits sigmoidally transformed cosine ("anti-logistic" extended cosinor) model Marler et al. (2006) averaged 24-hour activity profile. extended model relaxes symmetric shape ordinary cosinor adding two shape parameters: alpha sets relative width active versus rest phase, beta sets steepness rest--active transitions. model ActCR::ActExtendCosinor reproduces parameter estimates numerically.

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: list class "actiRhythm_cosinor_ext" containing: minimum Lower asymptote fitted curve (rest-phase level). amplitude Vertical span sigmoidal transition (amp); peak level minimum + amplitude. alpha Width-asymmetry parameter \([-1, 1]\). beta Steepness parameter (\(\ge 0\)). acrophase Time peak activity clock hours (acrotime). acrotime Alias acrophase (ActCR naming). UpMesor Clock time rest--active (rising) transition, \(-\arccos(\alpha) / (2\pi/T) + acrotime\). DownMesor Clock time active--rest (falling) transition, \(\arccos(\alpha) / (2\pi/T) + acrotime\). MESOR Midline statistic minimum + amplitude / 2. F_pseudo Pseudo-F improvement extended fit ordinary cosinor: \(((RSS_{cos} - RSS_{ext})/2)/(RSS_{ext}/(n-5))\). rss_cosinor Residual sum squares ordinary cosinor. rss_extended Residual sum squares extended cosinor. converged Logical; whether nonlinear fit converged. period Period used (hours). n_profile_hours Number profile hours used fit. non-convergence insufficient data numeric parameters returned NA converged = FALSE.

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: model fitted averaged profile $$f(t) = minimum + amplitude \cdot expit(\beta (cos(2\pi (t - acrotime)/T) - \alpha))$$ \(expit(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})\). ActCR/Marler parameterization, amplitude raw multiplier logistic transform (renormalized \(expit(\beta(1-\alpha))\)). closely related "normalized" Marler form, \(f(t) = min + amp \cdot expit(\beta(cos(\cdot) - \alpha)) / expit(\beta(1-\alpha))\), rescales amplitude peak equals exactly minimum + amplitude; two forms describe curve shape (alpha, beta, acrotime) parameters identical. normalized peak level reported peak. Starting values taken ordinary cosinor fit (minimum = max(mesor - amp, 0), amplitude = 2 * amp, alpha = 0, beta = 2, acrotime = ordinary acrophase). nonlinear least-squares problem solved stats::optim() using box-constrained L-BFGS-B method (bounds lower = c(0, 0, -1, 0, -3), upper = c(Inf, Inf, 1, Inf, 27), matching ActCR), extra package dependency required.

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: Marler MR, Gehrman P, Martin JL, Ancoli-Israel S (2006). sigmoidally transformed cosine curve: mathematical model circadian rhythms symmetric non-sinusoidal shapes. Statistics Medicine, 25(22):3893-3904. Wang J, et al. (2021). ActCR: Extract Circadian Rhythms Metrics Actigraphy Data. R package.

title: Compare Cosinor Rhythms Between Two Groups — cosinor.compare
code: set.seed(1); hrs <- 0:23 act <- ts <- subj <- grp <- NULL for (g in c("A", "B")) for (i in 1:5) {   acro <- if (g == "A") 8 else 11   y <- 100 + 40 * cos(2 * pi * (hrs - acro) / 24) + rnorm(24, 0, 4)   act <- c(act, y); subj <- c(subj, rep(paste0(g, i), 24)); grp <- c(grp, rep(g, 24))   ts <- c(ts, as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC") + hrs * 3600) } cosinor.compare(act, as.POSIXct(ts, tz = "UTC", origin = "1970-01-01"), subj, grp) #> Cosinor Comparison Between Two Groups #>  #>   Groups:  A (n=5)  vs  B (n=5) #>   Period:  24 h #>  #>   Joint (Bingham/Hotelling T2):  F(3,6) = 572.83   p = 9.2e-08 #>  #>   mesor     diff = +0.70   t(5.6) = 1.31   p = 0.243 #>   amplitude diff = -0.22   t(7.8) = -0.34   p = 0.743 #>   acrophase diff = -2.92   t(6.8) = -39.92   p = 2.77e-09

text: list class "actiRhythm_cosinor_ellipse" containing: ellipse Data frame columns x (\(\beta_1\)) y (\(\beta_2\)) giving ellipse boundary vertices. center Numeric vector c(beta1, beta2) point estimate. excludes_origin Logical; TRUE ellipse excludes (0, 0) (.e. rhythm detected requested level). rhythm_detected Alias excludes_origin. distance_stat Mahalanobis-type statistic origin relative fitted ellipse. critical_value Threshold \(2 F_{2, df, level}\) statistic compared . level Confidence level used. input missing required fields degenerate, boundary returned NA excludes_origin = NA.

title: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
text: cosinor model \(Y(t) = M + \beta_1 cos(\omega t) + \beta_2 sin(\omega t)\), \(100(1-\alpha)\%\) joint confidence region \((\beta_1, \beta_2)\) set points \(b\) satisfying $$(b - \hat b)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (b - \hat b) \le 2 F_{2, df, 1-\alpha}$$ \(\Sigma\) covariance estimated coefficients \(df\) residual degrees freedom. averaged-profile cosinor design used cosinor.analysis() orthogonal cosine sine columns, \(\Sigma\) (nearly) diagonal equal variances \(\sigma^2\); case se_amplitude (delta method) equals \(\sigma\), \(\Sigma\) reconstructed supplied explicitly. point estimate recovered amplitude acrophase \(\beta_1 = cos(\phi)\), \(\beta_2 = sin(\phi)\) \(\phi = acrophase \cdot 2\pi / T\).

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
text: Computes scaling exponent alpha activity time series using Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (Peng et al., 1994). DFA quantifies long-range temporal correlations: alpha approximately 0.5 indicates uncorrelated (white) noise, alpha approximately 1.0 indicates 1/f (pink) noise, alpha approximately 1.5 indicates Brownian (random-walk / brown) noise. Healthy human activity fluctuations typically show alpha 0.9 1.0 range, reductions reported aging Alzheimer's disease (Hu et al., 2009).

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
code: fractal.dfa(x, scale_min = 4, scale_max = NULL, breakpoint_min = 90)

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
text: x Numeric vector activity counts (minute-level recommended). Internally analyzed longest continuous non-NA segment. scale_min Integer. Smallest window size (box length) samples. Must >= 4 line can detrended residual degrees freedom. Default 4. scale_max Integer NULL. Largest window size samples. NULL (default) set floor(N / 4) N length analyzed segment, ensuring least four windows largest scale. breakpoint_min Numeric. Window-size boundary (samples / minutes minute-level data) separating short-timescale exponent alpha1 (scales < breakpoint_min) long-timescale exponent alpha2 (scales >= breakpoint_min). Default 90.

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
text: list class "actiRhythm_dfa" containing: alpha Overall scaling exponent: slope lm(log10(F) ~ log10(n)) across scales. alpha1 Short-timescale exponent (scales < breakpoint_min). NA fewer two qualifying scales. alpha2 Long-timescale exponent (scales >= breakpoint_min). NA fewer two qualifying scales. scales Integer vector window sizes n used. fluctuations Numeric vector fluctuation magnitudes F(n) corresponding scales. n_used Length analyzed (longest non-NA) segment. breakpoint_min breakpoint value used. unusable series (short, NA, constant) numeric scaling outputs returned NA structure (never error).

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
text: Algorithm (integrated, non-overlapping, linear DFA): Extract longest continuous non-NA segment x. Integrate mean-centred signal: y = cumsum(x - mean(x)). window size n (log-spaced scale_min scale_max), split y floor(N / n) non-overlapping windows length n, fit remove least-squares line within window, pool residuals. fluctuation F(n) = sqrt(mean(residuals^2)) pooled residuals. scaling exponent slope log10(F(n)) regressed log10(n). Window sizes unique integers chosen base-10 log grid, gives approximately even spacing log-log fit matches convention used reference implementations nonlinearTseries.

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) # White noise -> alpha near 0.5 fractal.dfa(rnorm(10000))$alpha #> [1] 0.4953119 # Brown noise -> alpha near 1.5 fractal.dfa(cumsum(rnorm(10000)))$alpha #> [1] 1.494906 # }

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
text: Computes Multiscale Sample Entropy (Costa et al., 2002, 2005), applies Sample Entropy (Richman & Moorman, 2000) coarse-grained versions activity time series across range temporal scales. MSE distinguishes genuinely complex signals (whose entropy sustained increases across scales, e.g. 1/f-like physiological signals) uncorrelated random signals (whose entropy falls monotonically scale).

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
text: x Numeric vector activity counts (minute-level recommended). Internally analyzed longest continuous non-NA segment. scales Integer vector coarse-graining scale factors tau. Default 1:20. m Integer embedding dimension (template length) Sample Entropy. Default 2. r Numeric tolerance fraction standard deviation ORIGINAL (scale-1) series. Chebyshev matching radius used every scale r * sd(x_original), following standard MSE convention holding r fixed absolute units across scales. Default 0.15.

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
text: list class "actiRhythm_mse" containing: mse Numeric vector SampEn values, one per requested scale (NA coarse-grained series short yields matches). scales Integer vector scale factors used. area Sum mse scales (complexity index), sum(mse, na.rm = TRUE). slope Slope lm(mse ~ scales) non-NA points (negative => entropy declines scale, typical noise). r_absolute absolute tolerance r * sd(x_original) used. n_used Length analyzed (longest non-NA) segment. unusable series entropy vector NA structure (never error).

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
text: scale tau series coarse-grained non-overlapping means length tau, Sample Entropy computed coarse-grained series embedding dimension m FIXED absolute tolerance r * sd(x_original). Sample Entropy negative natural log conditional probability two sub-sequences matching m points (within tolerance, Chebyshev distance, self-matches excluded) also match m + 1 points. implementation fully self-contained (base R / stats ).

title: Phase Concentration Tests — phase.concentration
text: Tests whether set daily phase markers (acrophases, onsets, L5/M10 times) concentrated rather scattered around clock. Reports mean resultant vector, Rayleigh test uniformity (Fisher 1993), Hermans-Rasson test (Hermans Rasson 2017), catches multimodal clustering Rayleigh misses.

text: Builds averaged 24-hour activity profile overlays two model fits comparison: Marler (2006) anti-logistic extended cosinor cosinor.antilogistic (drawn bold solid curve) ordinary single-component cosinor cosinor.analysis (drawn dashed curve). acrophase (time peak) marked vertical line extended-fit parameters shown subtitle.

title: Plot the Extended (Marler) Cosinor Fit on the 24-Hour Activity Profile — plot_extended_cosinor
text: ggplot object: averaged hourly profile (points light connecting line) hour--day 0-24, extended ordinary cosinor curves overlaid. extended fit converge profile ordinary cosinor still drawn "converge" note; insufficient data annotated empty ggplot returned. function never errors.

title: Plot the Extended (Marler) Cosinor Fit on the 24-Hour Activity Profile — plot_extended_cosinor
text: profile mean activity clock-hour bin averaged across recorded days (bin centres hour + 0.5), matching profile cosinor.analysis() cosinor.antilogistic() fit internally. fitted curves evaluated dense 0-24 h grid: Extended (Marler): \(f(t) = minimum + amplitude \cdot     expit(\beta (cos(2\pi (t - acrotime)/T) - \alpha))\), using minimum, amplitude, alpha, beta acrotime returned cosinor.antilogistic(). Ordinary: \(M + cos(2\pi (t - acrophase)/T)\), using mesor, amplitude acrophase cosinor.analysis(). subtitle reports extended-fit MESOR, amplitude, alpha width-asymmetry beta steepness fit converged.

title: Plot the Extended (Marler) Cosinor Fit on the 24-Hour Activity Profile — plot_extended_cosinor

title: Crespo Rest/Activity Period Detection — rest.crespo
text: Detects consolidated rest bouts across recording Crespo algorithm (Crespo et al. 2012): two-pass rank-order (median) filter mathematical-morphology pipeline turns activity counts binary rest/activity series reads every rest bout transitions. Like rest.periods returns number bouts, naps fragmented rest included; reaches result morphological filtering rather Roenneberg-style consolidation, two differ method.

Longer zero runs treated non-wear. t Quantile counts used replace non-wear epochs (default 0.33). alpha Expected daily rest length, seconds (default 28800, 8 hours); sets threshold percentile (alpha / 24 h) minimum data required. beta Filter morphology scale, seconds (default 3600, 1 hour); sets rank-order filter window structuring-element size.

title: Crespo Rest/Activity Period Detection — rest.crespo

(2012) #>

title: Sleep-Period-Time Window from the z-Angle (HDCZA) — rest.spt
text: Detects main sleep-period-time (SPT) window per day directly z-angle, sleep diary, using van Hees et al. (2018) Heuristic algorithm based Distribution Change Z-Angle (HDCZA): absolute 5-second change arm angle smoothed 5 minutes, thresholded fraction distribution, longest sustained low-change block per noon--noon day becomes SPT. raw-native: count-based equivalent.

code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 12:00", tz = "UTC"), by = 5, length.out = 17280) h <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")); night <- h >= 23 | h < 7 set.seed(1) anglez <- ifelse(night, -60, -30) + rnorm(17280, 0, ifelse(night, 0.02, 20)) spt <- rest.spt(anglez, ts, epoch_length = 5) sib <- sib.vanhees(anglez, epoch_length = 5) sleep.from.spt(spt, sib, ts, epoch_length = 5) #>         date               onset              offset      tst waso efficiency #> 1 2024-01-01 2024-01-01 23:00:05 2024-01-02 06:59:55 7.998611    0          1 #>   n_awakenings           mid_sleep #> 1            0 2024-01-02 03:00:00

title: Calculate Social Jet Lag — social.jet.lag
text: Social jet lag discrepancy social biological time, computed MSF - MSW (midpoint free days minus work days). MSFsc applies MCTQ sleep-debt correction (Roenneberg et al. 2012): free-day sleep exceeds work-day sleep (sleep catch-), free-day mid-sleep adjusted half excess weekly average sleep duration, SJLsc = MSFsc - MSW. based time bed (supplied -bed/-bed times), sleep onset offset. Positive values (common) indicate later sleep timing free days. Values > 1 hour associated increased health risks.

what: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: Computes Sleep Regularity Index exactly defined Phillips et al. (2017) using full epoch--day day concordance matrix. published form SRI. Unlike single fixed 24-hour-lag comparison, every epoch binned real clock time (epoch--day) calendar date, binary sleep/wake matrix built, index average agreement clock time CONSECUTIVE calendar days, rescaled interval [-100, 100].

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
code: sri.matrix(sleep_state, timestamps, epoch_length = 60)

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: sleep_state Sleep/wake state per epoch. Either character vector "S" (sleep) / "W" (wake), numeric/integer/logical vector 1/TRUE = sleep 0/FALSE = wake. NA marks unscored / non-wear epochs excluded concordance count. Must length timestamps. timestamps POSIXct vector epoch start times, one per element sleep_state. Need start midnight need perfectly contiguous; alignment real clock time. epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60). Must divide 86400 evenly; M = 86400 / epoch_length number epochs per day.

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: list components: SRI Numeric Sleep Regularity Index [-100, 100] (rounded 2 dp), NA_real_ computed. n_days Integer number distinct calendar days (N) spanned. n_valid_pairs Integer number consecutive-day epoch pairs actually compared (cells non-NA). method Character constant "phillips_matrix".

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: Let M = 86400 / epoch_length number epochs day let N number calendar days spanned recording. epoch assigned epoch--day index (1..M) clock time (hour/minute/second) day index j (1..N) calendar date. two indices populate binary matrix s[, j] 1 = sleep 0 = wake. SRI $$SRI = -100 + \frac{200}{M (N - 1)} \sum_{=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N-1}   \mathbf{1}\{ s_{,j} = s_{,j+1} \}$$ .e. proportion clock-time epochs hold state two consecutive days, mapped linearly perfect regularity scores +100, chance-level (independent) scoring 0, perfect anti-regularity -100. Robustness gaps partial wear: consecutive-day pair (, j) -> (, j + 1) either cell NA (missing epoch, non-wear, calendar day represented data) skipped counted. sum divided actual number valid pairs rather theoretical M * (N - 1), missing data reduce statistical power bias estimate. Days aligned true clock time using .POSIXlt() (hour, minute, second give epoch--day; calendar date gives day index), result correct even recording start midnight. SRI NA_real_ returned (never error) fewer two days, valid consecutive-day pairs, usable input. return structure always list.

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: Phillips AJK, Clerx WM, O'Brien CS, Sano , Barger LK, Picard RW, Lockley SW, Klerman EB, Czeisler CA (2017). Irregular sleep/wake patterns associated poorer academic performance delayed circadian sleep/wake timing. Scientific Reports, 7(1):3216. doi:10.1038/s41598-017-03171-4

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
code: # A perfectly regular sleeper: same 8h sleep block every day -> SRI near 100 ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 00:00:00", tz = "UTC"),           by = 60, length.out = 1440 * 4) tod <- as.POSIXlt(ts)$hour state <- ifelse(tod < 8, "S", "W")   # asleep 00:00-08:00 every day sri.matrix(state, ts, epoch_length = 60)$SRI #> [1] 100
